ambience/ocean_waves.description: Close field recording of medium ambient waves crushing on the shore of Kalundborg Fjord at Røsnæs. Water cascading through small stones as it pulls back into the ocean. Very distant vessel. Recorded at 2 meters distance, 120 degree with Zoom H2 build in microphones mid February.

ambience/crickets.description: Recording of a field cricket, Gryllus pennsylvanicus, that found its way into my garage a couple nights ago. (The cement floor and cinderblock wall probably accounts for the slight reverberation.) Location: Northeastern US. Since it was a warm night—about 85 ºF (29 ºC)—the chirping is fairly frequent. Recorded on a Sony Minidisc recorder with Sony ECM-MS907 Stereo Microphone, transferred to a Macintosh.  From the 1 minute+ master recording; I selected a 10-second soundbyte with the lowest level of ambient motor traffic noise. A high pass filter was used to remove background noise below 2.2 kHz; the major frequency of the song is about 4.7 kHz. The file was converted to ogg format.

ambience/campfire.description: Glaneur de sons: "Just a little fire that could be used to illustrate a summer camp?"

ambience/brook.description: Sound of water, recorded near a little brook, in a valley not far from Göreme (Turkey).

ambience/dawn_birds.description: This is a photo or sound file made in Utrechtse Heuvelrug National Park in the Netherlands, with the main subject of the file in the category: animals

ambience/rain.description:
Title Rain against the Window
Artist cori
Original mp3 data Length:1:21 minutes (1.25 MB) Format:MP3 Mono 44kHz 128Kbps (CBR)
User tags England, Rain, Weather, Wind, Window
Type Single Subject, Natural
PDSounds record number 600
Comment A typical English summer day ... the sound of rain lashing against the window pane and gusts of wind blowing at gale-force straight off the sea.  Recorded this morning by lying the mic on the window sill and uploaded without any audio changes other than a small amplification of the whole file.

env/boat_engine.description:
This hot bulb engine was recorded in action during a boat trip at Jäders bruk. The Seffle engine was built in 1950 by the Swedish Seffle engine company, and it still resides in its’ original position in a boat with a welded hull that operated in the Swedish logging trails. The starting handle on the flywheel is a bit tricky on the recording. When the engine starts it goes wrong way but by decreasing the speed it’s possible to turn the rotation of the engine.
Engine Speed: 500 rpm
Engine Power: 10 hp
Cast iron nr: 3639
Weight: 400 kg
Db:  87

Sound recordist: Torsten Nilsson
Photographer: Torsten Nilsson

Video recordist: Torsten Nilsson

env/wind_chime.description: Sound of a wind chime with bell rods and the eight audible tones a1-d2-f2-g2-a2-d3-f3-a3.

env/conch.artist: No machine-readable author provided. Dbolton assumed (based on copyright claims).
env/conch.description: Conch Shell recorded by David Bolton 18 February 2006 for use in w:Conch shell article

env/foghorn.description:
A foghorn is a fog signal that uses sound to warn vehicles of navigational hazards in foggy conditions. The term is most often used in relation to marine transport. 

This foghorn was used to help the fishing boats to navigate back to the harbour. It was operating when the visibility was lower than two nautical miles (3 704 meters). The foghorn house, in local dialect “Tudehuset”, was built 1959. Replacing an older hand driven foghorn installed 1933.
The foghorn was in use until beginning of 1990s.

A compressor delivers compressed air to a program control that regulates the signals.
The signal is a tone at about 360 Hertz and a sound circle of 60 seconds.

Signal Schedule:
Tone 1,5 seconds
Silence 2 seconds
Tone 4 seconds
Silence 52,5 seconds

118 dB recorded from 5 meters distance

Installed 1959
Sound recordist: Torsten Nilsson

Photographer: Torsten Nilsson

Comment Sounds of a splashes in a half full bathtub. The splashes were done inserting vertically a small plastic podium, so sometimes you can hear the bubbles going out from under the podium because I couldn't held it completely vertical.

env/jump.description: "Jump" by Gravity Sound — a real push-off / launch foley with a fast attack-and-decay transient (spectral centroid ~2.5kHz, pitch does NOT sweep up). Cropped from the 1.0s original to the ~0.34s core impact (leading ~0.18s of silence removed so the sound fires the instant the player leaves the ground), with 4ms/60ms in/out fades and peak normalization (see proc_jump.py). Same author as footstep / collect / whoosh / wind_forest, for a unified timbre. Replaces the prior 0.30s procedurally-synthesized sine-sweep 'hop' (gen_jump.py), whose upward whistle felt mismatched against the launch; sfx.ts playJump() keeps a matching warm-thump synth as the offline fallback.
